content.text: AWS offers a complete portfolio of storage services covering every access pattern: object storage for unstructured data, block storage for databases and operating systems, file storage for shared filesystems, and archival storage for compliance and cold data. Understanding the trade-offs between these types — durability, availability, cost, access patterns, and performance — is fundamental to AWS architecture.

attrs.alt: AWS storage service landscape showing S3 (object), EBS (block), EFS (file), S3 Glacier (archive), FSx (managed file systems), Storage Gateway (hybrid), Snow Family (physical transfer), and AWS Backup, with a decision guide and durability comparison

content.text: S3 (Simple Storage Service) is the most widely used AWS service. It stores objects (files) in buckets with no practical capacity limit — a single bucket can hold trillions of objects and exabytes of data. S3 is not a filesystem; objects are addressed by a key (path-like string), and you read or write the entire object at once via HTTP.

— 99.999999999% (11 nines) for S3 Standard, achieved by replicating objects across a minimum of 3 availability zones. Losing an S3 object from hardware failure is extraordinarily unlikely; the main risk is accidental deletion.

— S3 Standard ($0.023/GB), S3 Standard-IA ($0.0125/GB with retrieval fee), S3 One Zone-IA ($0.01/GB, single AZ), S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval, S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval, and S3 Glacier Deep Archive ($0.00099/GB). Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between tiers based on access frequency.

— automatically transition objects to cheaper storage classes after a set number of days (e.g., move to Standard-IA after 30 days, Glacier after 90 days, Deep Archive after 365 days, delete after 7 years).
— when enabled, every PUT creates a new version rather than overwriting. Deletions create a delete marker; previous versions are preserved. Essential for protecting against accidental deletion.
— S3 can trigger Lambda, SQS, or SNS on object creation/deletion events. This is the entry point for most data pipelines: file arrives in S3 → Lambda processes → result written back to S3.

import boto3

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

# Upload an object
s3.put_object(
    Bucket='my-data-bucket',
    Key='reports/2024/january.csv',
    Body=csv_bytes,
    ServerSideEncryption='aws:kms',
    StorageClass='STANDARD'
)

# Generate a pre-signed URL (share without requiring AWS credentials)
url = s3.generate_presigned_url(
    'get_object',
    Params={'Bucket': 'my-data-bucket', 'Key': 'reports/2024/january.csv'},
    ExpiresIn=3600   # valid for 1 hour
)
print(url)   # https://my-data-bucket.s3.amazonaws.com/...?X-Amz-Signature=...

S3 also hosts static websites (HTML, CSS, JS served directly from a bucket), acts as the origin for CloudFront distributions, and is the default destination for AWS service logs (CloudTrail, ELB access logs, VPC Flow Logs).

EBS (Elastic Block Store) provides block storage volumes that attach to EC2 instances as virtual disks. The operating system sees them as raw block devices and can format them with any filesystem (ext4, xfs, NTFS). Unlike S3, reads and writes to EBS go to specific bytes within the volume, making it suitable for databases and OS boot drives.

— gp3 (general purpose SSD, 3,000 IOPS baseline, recommended default), io2 Block Express (provisioned IOPS SSD, up to 256,000 IOPS for high-performance databases), st1 (throughput-optimized HDD for sequential reads, e.g., log processing), sc1 (cold HDD, lowest cost for infrequently accessed data).
— an EBS volume exists in a single AZ. It can only attach to EC2 instances in the same AZ. To move data to another AZ, take a snapshot, copy to the target AZ, and create a new volume.
— io1/io2 volumes can attach to up to 16 instances simultaneously (Multi-Attach), but the instances must use a cluster-aware filesystem; otherwise concurrent writes corrupt the data.

— point-in-time backups stored in S3. Incremental after the first. Snapshots can be copied across regions for disaster recovery. Restoring creates a new EBS volume that is immediately usable (data is loaded lazily from S3 in the background).

EFS provides a managed NFS (Network File System) that multiple EC2 instances, ECS tasks, EKS pods, and Lambda functions can mount simultaneously. Unlike EBS (single AZ, single instance by default), EFS is a Multi-AZ service with a POSIX-compliant filesystem interface. Capacity scales automatically — you never provision a size.
— General Purpose (default, lowest latency, up to 35,000 IOPS) and Max I/O (higher aggregate throughput for massively parallel workloads, slightly higher latency).

— Elastic (auto-scales throughput, recommended for variable workloads), Provisioned (set a fixed throughput independent of storage size), Bursting (proportional to storage size with burst credits — older model).
— EFS Standard ($0.30/GB/month) and EFS Infrequent Access ($0.025/GB/month + retrieval fee). Lifecycle policies move files to IA automatically after a period of no access.

— content management systems (WordPress uploads shared across multiple web servers), machine learning training data (shared across many GPU instances), CI/CD build caches, and home directories for development environments.

# Mount an EFS filesystem from an EC2 instance (using the EFS mount helper)
sudo yum install -y amazon-efs-utils
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/efs
sudo mount -t efs -o tls fs-0abc123def456789:/ /mnt/efs

# Or add to /etc/fstab for persistent mount
echo 'fs-0abc123def456789:/ /mnt/efs efs _netdev,tls 0 0' | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab

Glacier is the archive tier of S3, designed for data that is rarely or never accessed but must be retained for compliance, legal holds, or historical reference. The trade-off is retrieval latency and retrieval fees in exchange for dramatically lower storage costs.

Glacier data is placed there via S3 lifecycle rules — there is no separate Glacier API in the modern flow. Objects in Glacier appear in the S3 console with a storage class indicator; to retrieve one, initiate a restore operation, wait for it to complete (or pay for Expedited retrieval), then download within the restore window.
Amazon FSx — Managed Third-Party File Systems

FSx provides fully managed deployments of specific file systems that teams have standardized on outside AWS. Unlike EFS (a custom AWS NFS implementation), FSx products give you the exact protocol and feature set of the named system.
— SMB protocol, Active Directory integration, DFS namespaces, and shadow copies. The drop-in replacement for on-premises Windows file servers. Integrates with AWS Managed Microsoft AD.
— the Lustre parallel filesystem, used in HPC and ML training. Provides hundreds of GB/s of throughput. Can be linked to an S3 bucket, presenting S3 objects as Lustre files transparently.
— full ONTAP API and protocols (NFS, SMB, iSCSI). Supports thin provisioning, deduplication, compression, and snapshots. Ideal for enterprises migrating NetApp workloads to AWS.
— ZFS features (copy-on-write snapshots, data compression, clones) with NFS access. Good for workloads requiring ZFS-specific semantics.

Storage Gateway bridges on-premises applications with AWS cloud storage. A virtual appliance (or hardware appliance) is deployed on-premises and presents a local storage interface, while data is transparently tiered to S3 or Glacier in the background.
— presents S3 buckets as NFS or SMB mounts to on-premises servers. Files written to the mount are stored as S3 objects; frequently accessed files are cached locally.
— presents iSCSI block devices to on-premises servers. Cached mode keeps recently accessed data locally; stored mode keeps all data locally with asynchronous backup to S3.
— emulates a virtual tape library (VTL) compatible with existing backup software (Veeam, Veritas, etc.). Tapes are stored in S3 or Glacier, replacing physical tape infrastructure.
AWS Snow Family — Physical Data Transfer

When the volume of data to migrate exceeds what's practical over a network connection, the Snow Family provides physical devices shipped to your location. You copy data to the device, ship it back to AWS, and AWS imports it directly into S3.
— 8 TB usable (HDD) or 14 TB (SSD). Smallest form factor (fits in a backpack). Can also stream data online via DataSync when connectivity is available.
— Storage Optimized: 80 TB usable. Compute Optimized: 28 TB + GPU or EC2 compute for edge processing. Can run EC2 instances and Lambda functions locally.
— a 45-foot shipping container on a truck, providing 100 PB capacity. Used for single data center migrations at exabyte scale.

The network crossover point is roughly: if transferring data at your available bandwidth would take longer than 1 week, Snow devices are likely faster and cheaper. At 1 Gbps line speed, transferring 100 TB takes ~9 days; a Snowball Edge takes 5–7 business days total (ship, load, return, import).

AWS Backup provides a unified console and API for defining backup policies across EBS volumes, RDS databases, DynamoDB tables, EFS filesystems, FSx filesystems, and EC2 instances. Without Backup, you would configure snapshot schedules separately in each service.
— define schedules (hourly, daily, weekly), retention periods, and transition rules (move backups to cold storage after 30 days). Apply plans to resources via tags.
— automatically copy backups to another region or another AWS account for disaster recovery and ransomware protection.

— WORM (Write Once Read Many) protection for backup vaults. Once a vault lock is applied, backups cannot be deleted before their retention period expires — even by the root account. Required for HIPAA and PCI DSS compliance in many interpretations.

All AWS storage services support encryption at rest using AWS KMS. The default is AWS-managed keys (SSE-S3 for S3, aws/ebs for EBS, etc.) which is transparent and free. For compliance that requires customer control of key material, use Customer Managed Keys (CMK) in KMS.
— SSE-S3 (AES-256, AWS managed), SSE-KMS (CMK, audit trail in CloudTrail), SSE-C (customer provides key per request). Default encryption can be enforced at the bucket level.
— enable encryption at account level (encrypts all new volumes by default). Encrypted snapshots produce encrypted volumes; copying an unencrypted snapshot can apply encryption during the copy.
— encryption at rest and in transit (TLS mount). Enable at filesystem creation; cannot be enabled on an existing unencrypted filesystem in-place.

S3 has a layered access model: IAM policies control what principals can do, bucket policies add resource-based rules (useful for cross-account access or public access), and S3 Block Public Access settings act as a circuit breaker that overrides any ACL or bucket policy granting public access.

— four toggles (block public ACLs, ignore public ACLs, block public bucket policies, restrict public bucket policies). Enable all four at the account level via AWS Organizations SCP to prevent any S3 bucket from ever becoming public accidentally.

— a JSON resource-based policy attached to the bucket. Used for cross-account access (another AWS account's role accessing your bucket), enforcing HTTPS-only (deny HTTP requests), and restricting access to specific VPC endpoints.

— time-limited signed URLs that grant temporary access to a specific object without requiring the requester to have AWS credentials. The signature is generated server-side using the signer's IAM credentials.
— named endpoints that each carry their own policy, enabling different access rules for different consumers (data scientists vs. apps vs. analytics pipelines) from a single shared bucket.

S3 automatically partitions namespace based on key prefix, so well-distributed key names achieve higher throughput. A single S3 prefix supports 3,500 PUT/COPY/DELETE requests per second and 5,500 GET/HEAD requests per second.

— recommended for objects larger than 100 MB, required for objects over 5 GB. Splits the object into parts (5 MB minimum, 5 TB maximum), uploads them in parallel, and reassembles. Enables resumable uploads and bandwidth saturation.
— routes uploads through CloudFront edge locations over the AWS backbone instead of the public internet. Typically 50–500% faster for cross-region or international uploads.
— download only a specific byte range of an object with the HTTP
header. Allows parallel download of a single large object by requesting different ranges concurrently, then reassembling.

import boto3
from boto3.s3.transfer import TransferConfig

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

# Multipart upload with parallel threads
config = TransferConfig(
    multipart_threshold=100 * 1024 * 1024,   # 100 MB
    multipart_chunksize=10 * 1024 * 1024,    # 10 MB per part
    max_concurrency=10,                       # 10 parallel threads
    use_threads=True
)

s3.upload_file(
    'large-dataset.parquet',
    'my-data-bucket',
    'datasets/large-dataset.parquet',
    Config=config
)
Block device required for OS filesystem
Shared ML training data across many GPU EC2s
Multi-instance mount; Lustre for max throughput
Faster and cheaper than 10 Gbps upload

AWS DataSync automates moving large amounts of data between on-premises storage (NFS, SMB, HDFS, object storage) and AWS storage services (S3, EFS, FSx). It handles scheduling, encryption in transit, checksums, and bandwidth throttling.
— a DataSync agent (VM or container) is deployed on-premises. It connects to your storage and transfers data to AWS over TLS. No VPN required — the agent uses the public DataSync endpoint.
— configure hourly or daily sync tasks. DataSync transfers only changed data (delta transfers), making recurring sync efficient.
— can saturate 10 Gbps links (with parallel worker threads). Throttle during business hours to protect production traffic.
— DataSync also moves data between AWS storage services (EFS in one region to EFS in another, or S3 to EFS) — useful for multi-region data replication.

Amazon Data Lifecycle Manager (DLM) automates EBS snapshot creation and retention. Without DLM you must write custom Lambda functions or scripts to snapshot volumes on a schedule; DLM is a managed alternative requiring zero code.

Snapshots are incremental — only changed blocks since the last snapshot are written to S3. The first snapshot copies the entire volume; subsequent ones are very fast and cheap. You pay only for the changed blocks, not a full copy per snapshot.
S3 Intelligent-Tiering for Unknown Access Patterns

When you don't know how often objects will be accessed, S3 Intelligent-Tiering automatically moves objects between access tiers based on monitored access patterns. There are no retrieval fees and no minimum duration for the frequent/infrequent access tiers (only for archive tiers).
— objects accessed in the last 30 days. Same cost as S3 Standard ($0.023/GB).
— objects not accessed for 30 days. Moved automatically to $0.0125/GB. Moved back immediately on access.
— objects not accessed for 90 days. $0.004/GB. Retrieval still instant.
— $0.0025 per 1,000 objects per month. For objects smaller than ~128 KB, the monitoring cost exceeds the storage savings; use a lifecycle rule to Standard-IA instead.

S3 Object Lock implements WORM (Write Once Read Many) protection directly on S3 objects. Once locked, an object version cannot be overwritten or deleted until the retention period expires. This satisfies SEC Rule 17a-4, FINRA, CFTC, and similar financial compliance requirements.
— Governance mode (users with specific IAM permissions can override) vs Compliance mode (no one, including the root account, can delete before expiry — enforced at the AWS level).
— independent of retention period; protects objects indefinitely until the legal hold is explicitly removed. Used during litigation to preserve all relevant records.
— Object Lock requires bucket versioning. The lock applies to specific object versions, not the key itself.

s3 = boto3.client('s3')

# Lock an object version for 7 years in Compliance mode
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

retain_until = datetime.now(timezone.utc) + timedelta(days=365*7)

s3.put_object_retention(
    Bucket='compliance-bucket',
    Key='financial-records/2024/Q4.pdf',
    VersionId='3sL4kqtJlcpXrOhhygKhA5n...',
    Retention={
        'Mode': 'COMPLIANCE',
        'RetainUntilDate': retain_until
    }
)
— the leading cause of data breaches in the cloud. Enable S3 Block Public Access at the account level immediately. Use AWS Config rule s3-bucket-public-read-prohibited for continuous detection.
— an EBS volume and the EC2 instance must be in the same AZ. Creating a volume in us-east-1a and the instance in us-east-1b means the attachment fails.
— EFS Standard at $0.30/GB/month is 13× more expensive than EFS IA at $0.025/GB. Enable lifecycle transition to IA after 30 days; data is retrieved instantly on first access.
— snapshots persist after an instance or volume is deleted. Use AWS Config or a Lambda-based cleaner to identify and delete snapshots with no associated AMI or volume reference.
— placing an S3 bucket in a different region from the consuming EC2 instances incurs inter-region data transfer charges ($0.02/GB). Co-locate bucket and compute in the same region.

S3 is object storage for unstructured data with 11 nines durability; EBS is block storage attached to a single EC2 instance in one AZ; EFS is shared POSIX file storage spanning multiple AZs and instances.
S3 storage classes range from Standard ($0.023/GB) to Glacier Deep Archive ($0.00099/GB); lifecycle rules automate transitions based on age and access patterns.
FSx provides managed deployments of specific file system protocols (Windows SMB, Lustre HPC, NetApp ONTAP, OpenZFS) for workloads that require those exact APIs.

The Snow Family moves petabyte-scale data physically when network transfer would be impractical; Storage Gateway extends S3/Glacier into on-premises environments with familiar NFS/SMB/iSCSI interfaces.
All storage services support KMS encryption at rest and integrate with IAM for access control — apply encryption by default and use CMKs when compliance requires auditable key usage.
